context.title: Scaling Agentic Coding with Evaluator Loops
context.subtitle: How multi-agent architectures and context resets enable autonomous full-stack development.
context.summary: This article explores how a three-agent architecture—planner, generator, and evaluator—overcomes traditional AI ceilings in frontend design and autonomous coding. By using context resets and external skepticism, researchers can prevent models from losing coherence or self-praising mediocre work. This approach allows Claude to build complex, high-quality applications over multi-hour sessions without human intervention.

key_concepts.definition: The process of using AI models as independent workers that can plan, write, and test code autonomously.
The total amount of information an AI can 'remember' and process at one single time.
A model's tendency to rush or wrap up work prematurely as it nears its memory limit.
Clearing the AI's memory entirely and starting fresh with a structured summary of progress.
Summarizing previous parts of a conversation to save space without starting a new session.
A specific AI role dedicated to breaking down a large project into smaller, manageable tasks.
The AI responsible for the actual 'doing'—writing the code or creating the design.
An independent AI that reviews the generator's work against strict criteria to find flaws.
Structured documents that pass the necessary state and instructions from one agent session to the next.
The tendency for AI models to confidently praise their own work even when it is mediocre.
The ability of an AI to create visually appealing and functional user interfaces.
A system design where different AI instances work together, each handling a specific part of a workflow.

children.name: Solving Performance Ceilings
children.description: Moving beyond simple prompt engineering to break through quality plateaus.

children.name: Managing Context Limits
children.description: Strategies for keeping agents coherent during long-running tasks.

children.name: Ensuring Output Quality
children.description: Techniques for achieving human-level taste and functional correctness.

key_insights: A clean slate (Context Reset) is often more effective than summarizing history (Compaction) because it removes the 'anxiety' the model feels when its memory is full.
AI models are naturally bad at grading themselves; they are 'inclined to be generous' toward their own mistakes unless a separate skeptical agent is used.
Designing a high-quality evaluator is easier than designing a perfect generator, because skepticism is more 'tractable' to tune via prompting.
Success in long-running autonomous tasks requires shifting focus from prompt engineering to system harness design.

analogies.explanation: An author often misses their own typos or plot holes because they know what they meant to say; a separate editor sees the work with fresh, critical eyes.
Compaction is like reorganizing a pile of papers to make room, but a Context Reset is like clearing the desk entirely and only bringing back the essential notes you need for the next hour.
When a student sees there are only 5 minutes left on the clock, they stop doing their best work and start rushing to finish anything just to be done.
